title: 24 Nehase — The Arrival of Christ in Egypt & The Assembly
narrative:
Nehase

The Arrival of Christ in Egypt (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Eutyches the Apostolic Father and Martyr

St. Syra of Persia, Martyr

On the twenty-fourth day of Nehase, the Ethiopian Church commemorates monthly the Arrival of the Holy Family in Egypt — that sacred flight recorded by St. Matthew when Joseph, warned in a dream, took the child Jesus and His mother and fled before Herod's murderous edict. For the Ethiopian Church this is not merely a New Testament episode but a living geography: Egypt is the land of exile that became the land of formation, the country where the Patriarch Joseph was sold and rose to glory, where Moses was hidden and prepared, and where the infant Christ rested in holy ground already hallowed by the blood of the Israelite children. The Coptic tradition, the Ethiopian tradition's closest kin, traces every location in Egypt where the Holy Family rested, and those sites have been centers of pilgrimage, healing, and sanctity for two millennia.

The theological meaning of the Arrival of Christ in Egypt is dense and rich. The Prophet Hosea had spoken it centuries before: 'Out of Egypt I have called My Son' — a text that in its original context referred to the Exodus but that the Holy Spirit had packed with a deeper meaning that only the Incarnation would reveal. Christ enters Egypt as a refugee, a displaced child under the protection of a faithful foster-father and an unimaginably holy mother. He who made the cosmos accepted the indignity of exile. He who rules all kingdoms submitted to flight before a petty tyrant. The Arrival in Egypt is a concentrated image of the entire Incarnation — the Creator submitted to the conditions of the creature, the Mighty One accepting vulnerability, so that the vulnerable might receive His power.

St. Eutyches the Apostolic Father and Martyr — a disciple of the Apostle John and a companion of Polycarp and Ignatius — brings the thread of apostolic transmission into today's assembly. He is of the generation that received the Gospel from those who had received it directly from the Lord, and his martyrdom sealed the chain of testimony that runs from the eyewitnesses through the apostolic fathers to every subsequent generation. In today's context, his presence alongside the Arrival in Egypt speaks to the continuity of the holy story: the same Christ who fled to Egypt as an infant was proclaimed by Eutyches to his death, and is proclaimed today in the liturgies of the Ethiopian Church.

St. Syra of Persia, a martyr of the Sassanid persecution, adds the Persian witness to today's assembly — the Church beyond the borders of Rome, the Body of Christ in its most eastward reach, suffering under a different empire but with the same fidelity. The Holy Family fled west to Egypt; the Persian martyrs suffered east of the Euphrates. Christ, who arrived as a refugee in one foreign land, is worshipped in every land, and His martyrs fill every compass point. On this twenty-fourth day of Nehase, as the month nears its end, the Church holds the whole globe in its memory and gives thanks that the child who arrived in Egypt has filled the earth with His glory.
